title: Being prime minister is difficult, but not impossible
gist: Prospect editor Philip Collins argues UK political instability (seven PMs in a decade since Brexit) is rooted not in social media but in the material absence of earnings growth since 2008, and lays out a framework of three skills a PM needs — rhetoric, party/external politics, and administrative competence — judging Burnham strong on the first two but unproven on delivery.

insights.claim: The UK's revolving-door premiership (seven PMs in the decade since Brexit) is ultimately caused by the absence of earnings growth since the 2008 crash, which broke the postwar link between gentle economic progress and political stability.
insights.speaker: Philip Collins
insights.so_what: Frames UK political risk as a function of real-wage stagnation — a structural, not cyclical, instability that persists until growth returns.
insights.quote: Underneath that lies the cause of Brexit itself, which is the absence of earnings growth.
insights.locator: The proximate cause of the turmoil

insights.claim: Collins contends the real driver of political impatience is material rather than social media, arguing a Starmer government with 2.5% annual growth would face no resignation pressure regardless of WhatsApp chatter.
insights.speaker: Philip Collins
insights.so_what: Counters the consensus that social media drives instability — implies growth, not communications, is the binding constraint on UK governability.
insights.quote: Imagine a Starmer government with 2.5 per cent growth per annum. There is no prospect that the prime minister would be on the brink of resignation

insights.claim: Average UK workers have had no pay rise for a decade and a half, which Collins presents as the discontent that produced the Leave vote.
insights.speaker: Philip Collins
insights.so_what: Persistent real-wage stagnation is the macro backdrop sustaining UK populism and policy volatility.
insights.quote: when the average worker has not had a pay rise for a decade and a half
insights.locator: The proximate cause of the turmoil

themes.theme: Framework: three skills of a PM

insights.claim: Collins offers a framework that a good PM needs three rarely-combined virtues — rhetorical skill, management of internal/external party politics, and administrative competence to actually get things done.
insights.speaker: Philip Collins
insights.so_what: A reusable lens for assessing whether Burnham can deliver, not just communicate.
insights.quote: There are three aspects to being a good prime minister and it is rare to find all three virtues within one person.

insights.claim: Burnham has the communication gift Starmer lacked — sounding natural rather than calculated — and will handle the rhetorical and political dimensions of the job well.
insights.speaker: Philip Collins
insights.so_what: Burnham's communication edge supports the case for an early polling bounce.
insights.quote: Burnham undoubtedly has a gift of communication that his predecessor lacked.

insights.claim: The decisive open question is whether Burnham has the administrative competence to convert hopes into hard policy; if not, PM number eight since Brexit will follow quickly.
insights.speaker: Philip Collins
insights.so_what: Delivery, not charisma, is the swing factor for UK political stability over the next two years.
insights.quote: But what is he for? What will his government be like and does he have the ability to turn his hopes into hard policy?
insights.locator: Now it is Andy Burnham’s turn

insights.claim: Starmer's government acquired a reputation for having no purpose, which Collins identifies as the failure that puts a government into a precarious position even when it works hard.
insights.speaker: Philip Collins
insights.so_what: Purposelessness, not policy failure, is presented as the core political vulnerability — a warning sign to watch for in the successor.
insights.quote: A government which gains the reputation for having no purpose...will quickly fall into a precarious political position.
insights.locator: That disappointment is easier to bear
